speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 7 of the interview with Richard Pick in Mexico City, and I'd just like you just say a few closing words about your life after this tragedy and how you rebuilt.

speaker: interviewee
text: During all this time, I had been corresponding with Lore, who now is my wife. We knew each other from Germany. And lots of the correspondence was going on through Switzerland, because you couldn't correspond directly anymore after the United States and Mexico had gone officially into war-- declared war on Germany. So when I got out of the Army, Lore asked me, she said, why don't you visit me here in Mexico. I was in New York. I was discharged in December 1945. And I said, all right. I come to Mexico, and I came to Mexico, and we got engaged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we planned to get married in-- in January. Six months later, I came here in I think it was in June or July of '46. We got married in July of 47.

speaker: interviewee
text: January.

speaker: interviewee
text: In January of '47. I'm sorry-- January 16, '47. And Lore, at the time, she was sewing. She was sewing at home with an old Singer Sewing machine, with a pedal-- sewing machine. And she was earning the money for her family, for her parents, because her father was at that time already was, I think probably 65 or 70 years old. And he didn't know any Spanish. Her mother knew hardly any Spanish, and they just couldn't make ends meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so Lore, she took care of the family, economically speaking. And when we got married, I-- I didn't want to stay here. I mean, Mexico to me-- it just was so different. And I said let's, go to New York. And she said, listen, I have my family here, you know. Why don't you give it a try? If you don't like it after one year, I go with you back to New York. I said, all right. Let's try it, and we tried it. And now it's 50 years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked it a little bit better. And then we started manufacturing dresses. We cut in our dining-- little dining room table with scissors. We got blisters on our-- on our hands because we only could cut two or three dresses at one time, layers, you know. And we put rags around the scissors, and we-- we got someone with a [INAUDIBLE] after a few months, who we rented a sewing machine, but we could only rent it during the night because, he used it during the day. So we rented it from, I don't know, from 8:00 to 8:00 in the morning. Then we had to bring it back, so we would work during the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started out--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was pretty rough at the beginning, but we made it go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a family. We have two daughters, and-- Susan and Sylvia. And Susan has two boys and one girl, 18, 16, and 12. And Sylvia, Sylvia has a boy and a girl. They are 11 and 9. And professionally, Susan has a PhD from the University of London in social psychology and she is working in her profession here in Mexico. She is very active. So is Sylvia. She is a gynecologist. She has her office, her practice, together with two other gynecologists at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you shared your story with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to the extent I've shared it with you, no, because I'll tell you my children, they-- they have heard a lot about a Holocaust. And I don't know. They appear to be me they want to keep away a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: For them, it's just too sad. For them, it's like when my grandfather told me about Napoleon, you know? But it's-- I must say it's very unfortunate, because I have-- I would very much like to talk to them about it, but I have never been able to do this, just a hint here and a hint there and photographs, or-- but they have never heard it, and I must even say seen it, to the extent you have. Well, I think you've done your family and the world a great service by sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so too. I think I am really looking forward to get this audio tape, because then I am pretty sure that my family will sit down and would look at it, and they will get the full story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll look at some photos and documents now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain who is in the photo, when it was taken, and where, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo was taken around 1902, about 90 years ago. And on the right is my grandmother, Lina Baum, and the girl standing in front is my mother. The one on the left is her brother, who died in the First World War as a soldier for Germany. He gave his life while they killed his mother in the-- later on in Auschwitz. And another sister on top on the left was also killed in Theresienstadt. So of the six persons you see here, actually, four were killed in different concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken around 1912. This is grandma, my paternal grandmother, Anna Pick, and her children. And of the six people you see here, again, four were killed in different concentration camps, including my grandmother, Anna Pick.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and my father. This was taken around 1940 or '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, hold on. I just want to check the focus. Hold on 10 seconds. You can explain again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me when I should start.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and my father taken in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Emma Pick, nee Baum, and Paul Pick. Here to the right, you see the interior of the old and beautiful synagogue in Stuttgart, which was burned and completely destroyed during the Kristallnacht. And on the left, you see the little house on the main square on the Marktplatz, Marktplatz 19 in Stuttgart, when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Gestapo put out the swastika flag on Hitler's 50th birthday when he came to Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, you see our family, my father, my mother, my brother, Larry, my grandmother, Anna, and myself. The picture was taken around 1932. In the center, you see the sister of my father, my aunt, with a little-- with her little child, Renee. They were both killed in concentration camps. The child at the time was seven or eight years old when she was killed. And the story about my mother and father, well, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling again.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on top is a view of Stuttgart when I came to Stuttgart the first time, in April, 1945. It was just one pile of rubble, and the one on the bottom is one of those announcements which was put all over the city and in other cities too, of course, by the Allied forces to show the Germans what they did in the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do the words mean in English? [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] These atrocities, you are-- you are to blame-- your blame. These atrocities, your blame.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, Lina Baum, with whom I lived for a few years in Stuttgart before my immigration. She was deported to Theresienstadt and into Auschwitz, from where she was deported to Birkenau, and the same day she arrived in Birkenau, she was killed in a gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We are running.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lore and myself, believe it or not. But of course, this picture was taken probably 45 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mexico City.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Mexico City, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on, please. You were having a great time, weren't you? Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the right is my brother, Larry. Of course, this picture was taken just a couple of years ago. And the one on the left is my family. On the top left is Sylvia, the top right is Susan, and then the five grandchildren of the left is Gabi. At the bottom is Danny. Then going up in the middle row is Alex, and then Sonia, and then Arturo, and on the right is Susan. And we love them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll do it as a private project.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the thing. I mean, I don't think that--
